questions.prompt: When should you use Alpine vs Debian-slim as a base?

choices.text: Alpine for simple apps and statically-compiled workloads where its 5 MB base wins on size. Debian-slim for apps with native dependencies compiled against glibc — NumPy, Pandas, many Node native modules, anything with prebuilt wheels. Alpine uses musl libc (not glibc), so glibc-compiled binaries either don't run or require compilation from source (slow, sometimes broken). Distroless variants beat both for production runtime images. Pin the version (e.g., alpine:3.19, not alpine:latest) for reproducibility.

questions.explanation: Alpine for static/simple; Debian-slim for native-dep workloads; distroless beats both for production minimal runtime.

questions.prompt: A Go service ships in a 1 GB image. What's the minimum viable size?

choices.text: 5-20 MB. Multi-stage build: golang base for builder, then `FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot` (or scratch) for runtime with just the static binary copied in. CGO_ENABLED=0 for static linking; strip debug symbols with -ldflags='-s -w'. Result: tiny image, no shell for attackers, fast pulls, minimal attack surface. Standard pattern for any statically-compiled language.

questions.explanation: Multi-stage + distroless/scratch + static binary = ~10-20 MB Go images. Universal pattern for static binaries.
